stem: In the $xy$-plane, a student graphs the lines $y = 3x + 2$ and $y = x + 8$. What are the coordinates of the point where the two lines intersect?
explanation:
At the intersection point, both equations have the same $x$- and $y$-values. Setting the right-hand sides equal:
$$3x + 2 = x + 8$$
$$3x - x = 8 - 2$$
$$2x = 6$$
$$x = 3$$

Back-substituting into the second equation:
$$y = 3 + 8 = 11$$

Checking in the first equation: $y = 3(3) + 2 = 9 + 2 = 11$ ✓
Checking in the second equation: $y = 3 + 8 = 11$ ✓

The intersection point is $\boxed{(3, 11)}$.

distractorRationale.A: Coefficient-drop error when back-substituting: the student correctly finds $x = 3$ but evaluates the first equation as $y = x + 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$ (drops the coefficient of 3 from $3x$), giving the point $(3, 5)$.
distractorRationale.C: Sign-flip error when moving terms: the student writes $3x + 2 = x + 8 \Rightarrow 3x - x = 8 + 2 = 10$ (adds 2 to the right instead of subtracting it), giving $2x = 10 \Rightarrow x = 5$; back-substituting into $y = 3x + 2$ yields $y = 3(5) + 2 = 17$, producing the point $(5, 17)$.
distractorRationale.D: Forgot-to-divide error: the student correctly obtains $2x = 6$ but reports $x = 6$ (forgetting to divide by 2); back-substituting gives $y = 6 + 8 = 14$, yielding the point $(6, 14)$.
